docs.title: Random Notes
docs.text:
This section contains a bunch of notes on random subjects that I take from time to time. It helps me keep track of various things I work on when I pick them up after a long break.

Look them up, you may find something useful, you never know .

Note

The list is currently unsorted.

docs.title: Tags
docs.text: Following is a list of relevant tags used across notes:

docs.title: Concurrency
docs.text: • Causal chains and event ordering in distributed systems

docs.title: Website
docs.text: • Creating a Personal Website

docs.title: Creating a Personal Website
docs.text: I wanted a personal website that was simple to write for, easy to maintain, and boring to deploy. This note captures the setup I use for this site: Markdown content, MkDocs for generation, Material for MkDocs for the theme, and GitHub Pages for hosting.

docs.title: Static site generator
docs.text:
A static site generator is a good fit for a personal website. There is no server to run, no database to maintain, and no admin UI to keep updated. The site is just source files in Git, and the generated output is static HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

There are multiple good options for this:

  • Jekyll
  • MkDocs
  • Hugo
  • Docusaurus

This repository uses MkDocs with Material for MkDocs. MkDocs is Python based, configured with YAML, and pleasant enough for a notes-first website. Material adds the parts I do not want to build myself: responsive layout, search, navigation, dark mode, tags, and decent defaults.

docs.title: Local setup
docs.text:
The Python dependencies are pinned in requirements.txt:

mkdocs==1.6.1
mkdocs-material==9.7.6
mkdocs-git-revision-date-localized-plugin==1.5.1
mkdocs-glightbox==0.5.2
cairosvg==2.9.0
pillow==12.2.0

The Makefile keeps the common commands short.

make setup

To run the site locally:

make run

This starts mkdocs serve, which serves the website locally and reloads when content changes.

To build the site once:

make build

This runs mkdocs build and writes the generated site to the site/ directory.

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
The writing flow is intentionally plain. Pages are Markdown files under docs/. The home page is docs/index.md, the about page is docs/about.md, and notes live under docs/notes/ as dated Markdown files.

For example, this note is:

docs/notes/20230527.md

A note starts with front matter for tags:

---
tags:
  - Website
---
# Creating a Personal Website

Tags are configured in mkdocs.yml, and the tag index is generated through docs/notes/index.md.

Images are kept under docs/assets/. The home page profile image, for example, is stored at:

docs/assets/images/profile.jpg

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
The site-level configuration lives in mkdocs.yml.

The basic metadata is:

site_name: Utkarsh Saxena
site_url: https://utk-spartan.github.io/
site_description: Personal website of Utkarsh Saxena
site_author: Utkarsh Saxena
docs_dir: docs/

The site uses the Material theme with navigation tabs, section navigation, search suggestions, search highlighting, dark and light palettes, and social links.

Useful plugins enabled in this repo:

  • search, for local site search
  • git-revision-date-localized, for page creation and update dates
  • tags, for browsing notes by topic
  • glightbox, for image previews
  • social, for social preview cards in CI

docs.title: GitHub Pages
docs.text:
GitHub Pages is enough for this kind of site. The repository has a GitHub Actions workflow at .github/workflows/main.yml that deploys the site when changes are pushed to master.

The trigger is:

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - master

And the deployment steps are:

  1. checks out the repository
  2. sets up Python
  3. installs dependencies with make setup
  4. deploys the site with mkdocs gh-deploy --force

mkdocs gh-deploy builds the static site and publishes the generated output to the GitHub Pages branch.

The live site URL is:

https://utk-spartan.github.io/

docs.title: Google analytics
docs.text:
Analytics is optional. I have kept the Material for MkDocs analytics section in mkdocs.yml, but commented out:

# analytics:
#   provider: google
#   property:

To enable Google Analytics, create a Google Analytics property and add the property ID there.

docs.title: Google search indexing
docs.text:
Once the site is deployed, it can be added to Google Search Console using the site URL:

https://utk-spartan.github.io/

After verification, submit the generated sitemap:

https://utk-spartan.github.io/sitemap.xml

MkDocs generates the sitemap during the build, so no custom sitemap file is needed for the basic setup.

docs.title: In-memory non-relational database on 2D arrays
docs.text:
This is a small project idea: build an in-memory, non-relational database in Haskell where the storage layer is deliberately simple, but the transaction layer is interesting.

The goal is not to build a production database. The goal is to understand how basic storage, transactions, and isolation levels interact when the whole system is small enough to hold in your head.

docs.title: Define Scope
docs.text:
The database stores tables as 2D arrays. Each table is a grid of rows and columns, and each cell stores a value.

The first version should support:

  • creating a table with a fixed schema
  • inserting rows
  • reading rows by row id
  • updating cells
  • deleting rows
  • starting, committing, and aborting transactions
  • running the same operations under different isolation levels

The first version should not support:

  • SQL parsing
  • indexes
  • joins
  • persistence
  • query planning
  • distributed execution
  • secondary replicas

Keeping these out of scope is important. The interesting part here is the transaction behavior, not building every feature expected from a database.

docs.title: Setup skeleton and user interfaces
docs.text:
The project can start with a small library and a thin REPL or CLI around it. The library should expose the database operations directly, and the CLI can exist only to make manual testing easier.

The rough API can look like this:

createTable :: TableName -> [Column] -> Txn ()
insertRow    :: TableName -> [Value] -> Txn RowId
readRow      :: TableName -> RowId -> Txn (Maybe Row)
updateCell   :: TableName -> RowId -> ColumnName -> Value -> Txn ()
deleteRow    :: TableName -> RowId -> Txn ()

Transaction execution should make the isolation level explicit:

runTxn :: IsolationLevel -> Database -> Txn a -> IO (Either TxnError a, Database)

The CLI can stay minimal:

begin serializable
insert users ["utkarsh", 25]
read users 1
update users 1 age 26
commit

This gives enough surface area to test reads, writes, conflicts, and rollback behavior without implementing a full SQL layer.

docs.title: Define data models
docs.text:
The storage model can be intentionally boring.

newtype TableName = TableName Text
newtype ColumnName = ColumnName Text
newtype RowId = RowId Int
newtype TxnId = TxnId Int

data Value
  = VInt Int
  | VText Text
  | VBool Bool
  | VNull

data Column = Column
  { columnName :: ColumnName
  , columnType :: ColumnType
  }

data Row = Row
  { rowId  :: RowId
  , values :: Vector Value
  }

data Table = Table
  { schema :: Vector Column
  , rows   :: Vector (Maybe Row)
  }

data Database = Database
  { tables :: Map TableName Table
  }

Using Vector (Maybe Row) keeps row ids stable after deletion. A deleted row can be represented as Nothing, and a later compaction pass can be avoided in the first version.

The transaction layer needs extra state:

data IsolationLevel
  = ReadUncommitted
  | ReadCommitted
  | RepeatableRead
  | Serializable

data TxnState = TxnState
  { txnId       :: TxnId
  , isolation   :: IsolationLevel
  , readSet     :: Set CellRef
  , writeSet    :: Map CellRef Value
  , snapshot    :: Maybe Database
  }

CellRef can identify a single cell:

data CellRef = CellRef TableName RowId ColumnName

For a toy implementation, row-level references may be easier than cell-level references. Cell-level tracking is more precise, but row-level tracking is simpler and still useful for learning the isolation behavior.

docs.title: Implement each isolation level in phases
docs.text:
The four classic isolation levels are:

  1. Read uncommitted
  2. Read committed
  3. Repeatable read
  4. Serializable

Each level restricts more behavior than the one before it.

docs.title: Phase 1: read uncommitted
docs.text:
This is the weakest isolation level. A transaction can read data written by another transaction before that transaction commits.

Implementation approach:

  • apply writes directly to shared in-memory state
  • keep an undo log for rollback
  • allow reads to see the latest value, even if it is uncommitted

This phase is useful because it exposes the problem clearly. Dirty reads are possible by design:

  1. transaction A updates a value
  2. transaction B reads that value
  3. transaction A aborts
  4. transaction B has observed a value that never committed

docs.title: Phase 2: read committed
docs.text:
Read committed prevents dirty reads. A transaction should only read committed data.

Implementation approach:

  • keep transaction writes private until commit
  • reads consult the committed database state
  • commit applies the transaction write set atomically

This can be implemented with a global commit lock at first. That is not the most concurrent design, but it keeps the correctness model simple.

Read committed still allows non-repeatable reads:

  1. transaction A reads a row
  2. transaction B updates and commits the same row
  3. transaction A reads the row again and sees a different value

docs.title: Phase 3: repeatable read
docs.text:
Repeatable read ensures that if a transaction reads the same row twice, it sees the same value both times.

Implementation approach:

  • take a snapshot of the committed database when the transaction starts
  • serve reads from that snapshot
  • keep writes in the transaction write set
  • on commit, check for write-write conflicts

This gives a simple MVCC-like model. Reads are stable because they come from the snapshot. Writes are validated before they become visible.

This level can still allow some anomalies depending on the exact implementation, especially predicate-related anomalies. Since this toy database does not support indexes or predicates initially, row-level repeatability is enough for the first version.

docs.title: Phase 4: serializable
docs.text:
Serializable is the strongest level. The outcome should be equivalent to running committed transactions one at a time in some order.

Implementation approach:

  • track the read set and write set for each transaction
  • validate at commit time that no committed transaction changed data read by the current transaction
  • abort and retry when validation fails

This is optimistic concurrency control. The transaction runs without taking heavy locks, and commit decides whether the transaction was safe.

A transaction should fail validation if:

  • another transaction committed a write to something in its read set
  • another transaction committed a conflicting write to something in its write set

The simplest implementation can use a global version counter. Every committed cell or row stores the version at which it was last modified. A transaction records the database version it started from. During commit, it checks whether any row it read was modified after that start version.

docs.title: Testing the behavior
docs.text:
The tests should focus on anomalies, not just CRUD behavior.

Examples worth testing:

  • dirty read is possible under read uncommitted
  • dirty read is not possible under read committed
  • repeated reads can change under read committed
  • repeated reads stay stable under repeatable read
  • conflicting serializable transactions cannot both commit
  • aborting a transaction does not leak its private writes

Small deterministic tests are better than a large random test suite at the start. Once the semantics are clear, property tests can be added for invariants like row id stability, rollback correctness, and serializable validation.

docs.title: Why Haskell
docs.text:
Haskell is a good fit for this experiment because the boundary between pure state transitions and effectful execution can be kept explicit. Most of the database logic can be modeled as transformations over immutable values, while the outer shell handles concurrency, IO, and the REPL.

That separation should make it easier to test the core transaction logic without having to spin up an external service or rely on timing-heavy tests.

docs.title: Causal chains and event ordering in distributed systems
Causal chains and event ordering in distributed systems
docs.text:
Distributed systems make ordering difficult. On a single machine, it is tempting to think of events as happening in one clear sequence. Across machines, that sequence does not really exist.

Clocks drift, messages take different paths, network delays vary, and two events can happen independently without either one being "first" in a meaningful way. For many systems, the useful question is not "which event happened earlier by wall-clock time?", but "could this event have influenced that event?"

That is the idea behind causal ordering.

docs.title: Logical clock algorithms
docs.text:
A logical clock is a way to assign timestamps to events without depending on physical time.

Instead of asking machines to agree on the current time, each process maintains some metadata that moves forward as events happen. That metadata is attached to messages and merged when messages are received.

Logical clocks are useful because they describe ordering from the system's own execution, not from an external clock source.

The most common models are:

  • Lamport timestamps
  • vector clocks

They answer slightly different questions.

docs.title: Lamport timestamps
docs.text:
Lamport timestamps give every event a monotonically increasing number.

Each process keeps a counter:

  1. increment the counter before every local event
  2. attach the counter value to every outgoing message
  3. when receiving a message, set the local counter to max(local, received) + 1

This gives a simple ordering rule:

event A happened-before event B => timestamp(A) < timestamp(B)

The important detail is that the reverse is not guaranteed. If timestamp(A) < timestamp(B), it does not necessarily mean A caused B. Lamport timestamps can tell us that one event may be ordered before another, but they cannot reliably distinguish causality from unrelated timing.

Example:

Process P1: A -------- send message -------- C
                         |
                         v
Process P2: B -------- receive message ----- D

Here, A causally influences D if the message sent after A is received before D. But B may be completely independent of A, even if its Lamport timestamp is larger.

Lamport clocks are still useful. They are small, cheap, and enough when a total or partial processing order is more important than detecting concurrency.

docs.title: Vector clocks
docs.text:
Vector clocks preserve more information.

Instead of a single counter, each process keeps a map from process id to counter:

P1: { P1: 3, P2: 1, P3: 0 }

The rules are:

  1. increment your own entry before every local event
  2. attach the whole vector clock to outgoing messages
  3. on receive, take the element-wise maximum of the local and received clocks
  4. increment your own entry after merging

Vector clocks let us compare two events:

  • A < B if every component of A is less than or equal to B, and at least one component is strictly less
  • A == B if all components are equal
  • A || B if neither clock is less than the other

The last case means the events are concurrent. Neither event causally depends on the other.

Example:

A = { P1: 2, P2: 0 }
B = { P1: 2, P2: 3 }

A < B, because B has seen everything A had seen, plus more from P2.

But:

A = { P1: 2, P2: 0 }
B = { P1: 1, P2: 3 }

These are concurrent. A knows more about P1, while B knows more about P2.

docs.title: Causal chains of events
docs.text:
A causal chain is a sequence of events where each event could have influenced the next one.

The basic happened-before relation is:

  • events on the same process are ordered by local execution
  • sending a message happens before receiving that message
  • the relation is transitive

So if:

A happened-before B
B happened-before C

then:

A happened-before C

This matters in real systems. For example:

  • replicated databases need to know whether two writes conflict or whether one supersedes the other
  • chat systems may want replies to appear after the messages they refer to
  • distributed logs need to separate causal order from arrival order
  • caches and materialized views need to avoid applying stale updates over newer ones

Wall-clock timestamps are often not enough for these cases. A message can arrive late with an older physical timestamp but still carry causally important state.

docs.title: Implement vector clock in scala
docs.text:
The core data structure can be small.

final case class NodeId(value: String) extends AnyVal

final case class VectorClock private (versions: Map[NodeId, Long]) {
  def increment(node: NodeId): VectorClock = {
    val next = versions.getOrElse(node, 0L) + 1L
    copy(versions = versions.updated(node, next))
  }

  def merge(other: VectorClock): VectorClock = {
    val allNodes = versions.keySet ++ other.versions.keySet

    val merged = allNodes.map { node =>
      val left = versions.getOrElse(node, 0L)
      val right = other.versions.getOrElse(node, 0L)
      node -> math.max(left, right)
    }.toMap

    VectorClock(merged)
  }

  def compare(other: VectorClock): ClockOrdering = {
    val allNodes = versions.keySet ++ other.versions.keySet

    val comparisons = allNodes.map { node =>
      val left = versions.getOrElse(node, 0L)
      val right = other.versions.getOrElse(node, 0L)
      java.lang.Long.compare(left, right)
    }

    val hasLess = comparisons.exists(_ < 0)
    val hasGreater = comparisons.exists(_ > 0)

    (hasLess, hasGreater) match {
      case (false, false) => ClockOrdering.Equal
      case (true, false)  => ClockOrdering.Before
      case (false, true)  => ClockOrdering.After
      case (true, true)   => ClockOrdering.Concurrent
    }
  }
}

object VectorClock {
  val empty: VectorClock = VectorClock(Map.empty)
}

sealed trait ClockOrdering

object ClockOrdering {
  case object Before extends ClockOrdering
  case object After extends ClockOrdering
  case object Equal extends ClockOrdering
  case object Concurrent extends ClockOrdering
}

A send event increments the sender clock and attaches it to the message:

val p1 = NodeId("p1")

val clockAfterSend =
  VectorClock.empty
    .increment(p1)

A receive event merges the incoming clock and then increments the receiver:

val p2 = NodeId("p2")

val receiverClock =
  VectorClock.empty
    .merge(clockAfterSend)
    .increment(p2)

Now the receiver event is causally after the sender event:

clockAfterSend.compare(receiverClock)
// ClockOrdering.Before

Concurrent events look different:

val a = VectorClock.empty.increment(NodeId("p1"))
val b = VectorClock.empty.increment(NodeId("p2"))

a.compare(b)
// ClockOrdering.Concurrent

docs.title: Trade-offs
docs.text:
Vector clocks are more expressive than Lamport timestamps, but they are also larger. The timestamp grows with the number of participating nodes.

That is acceptable for small clusters, actor systems, CRDT experiments, and debugging tools. For very large systems, vector clocks need pruning, version vectors, dotted version vectors, or a different causality-tracking model.

The useful lesson is still the same: physical time is not the same as causal time. Once multiple machines are involved, event ordering becomes part of the data model.
